behaviors.description: The content passed to the summary-generation step includes data retrieved from all three sources.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module handoff: source clients/adapters + aggregation/orchestration + summary-input builder. This is not just single-function logic because the risk is one source payload being dropped or mis-mapped during cross-module wiring. REAL-INFRA is not required (in-process fakes for the three sources are sufficient), no LLM-output-quality dependence, and no multi-agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real aggregation/orchestration modules with fake source adapters (metrics, issues, deploy logs) returning distinct sentinel fields. Assert the exact payload handed to summary-generation contains all three sections and expected key fields. Concrete lower-level miss: a unit test of only the payload-builder with pre-normalized fixtures would miss an adapter-to-aggregator schema mismatch that drops one source before handoff.

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, the report is produced with a `data unavailable` note for that section rather than aborting.
behaviors.justification: This is cross-module error propagation and fallback rendering: source failure handling + report composer behavior. In-process failure injection (e.g., one source client raises/returns error) is enough; no real OS/network/subprocess is necessary for the specific failure mode. No dependence on model judgment and no multi-step agentic sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Run the report pipeline modules in-process with one source adapter failing and the other two succeeding. Assert job completion, section-level `data unavailable` note for the failed source, and inclusion of remaining sections. Concrete lower-level miss: unit-testing only fallback formatter would miss an uncaught exception at orchestrator boundary that aborts before fallback note is emitted.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on model output quality (faithfulness to input values). A deterministic stub can always pass and cannot validate real model decisions; this is the deterministic-assertion trap. Not Workflow because behavior is a single agentic generation step, not multiple agentic stages in sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Execute summary generation against the real model on representative inputs with known key values and assert factual consistency criteria (key numbers/issues/deploy outcomes preserved, no contradictions). Run repeated trials and track violation rate threshold. Concrete lower-level miss: an integration test with canned model output would never catch hallucinated or swapped values from the real model.

behaviors.description: A complete report — from data collection through email delivery — reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly.
behaviors.justification: End-to-end path includes summary generation plus delivery. Because execution includes real model generation, stubbed summaries can hide real failures caused by actual model output characteristics (length/format/content affecting downstream rendering/delivery). System-only would miss those model-dependent failures. Not Workflow: only one agentic decision point is implied.
behaviors.test_description: Run full pipeline against real dependencies (or production-like endpoints) including real summary generation and actual email delivery to a test inbox. Assert email arrival, recipient correctness, and report completeness in body/attachment. Concrete lower-level miss (System): with a fixed stub summary, test passes, but real model output can trigger template/encoding/size edge cases that break final delivery or corrupt the delivered report.

behaviors.description: When the email delivery service is unreachable, the generated report is saved to a local file and delivery is retried on the next scheduled run.
behaviors.justification: Behavior depends on real infrastructure boundaries: email service unreachability, local filesystem persistence across runs, and next scheduled execution semantics. In-process mocks can hide process-boundary and filesystem realities. No LLM-output-quality requirement and no multi-agentic pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Execute run 1 with email endpoint intentionally unreachable; assert report is persisted to disk at configured path. Execute run 2 as a separate scheduled invocation and assert persisted report is retried/sent and state is updated/cleared correctly. Concrete lower-level miss (Integration): in-memory or mocked persistence/retry can pass while real separate-process run fails to reload persisted artifact (path/permissions/serialization mismatch).
